coredata.dc:title: Ti3C2Tx MXene@carbon dots hybrid microflowers as a binder-free electrode material toward high capacity capacitive deionization
coredata.dc:creator: Tan, Zhiyou
Wang, Wang
Zhu, Mengke
Liu, Yucheng
Yang, Yixia
Ji, Xinghu
He, Zhike
coredata.dc:description: Owing to the exceptional electrical conductivity and high hydrophilic surface area, MXene is regarded as one of the most glaring capacitive deionization (CDI) electrode materials. However, the self-stacking of pure MXene nanosheets though van der Waals forces seriously hinders its electrochemical performance. Therefore, a novel pathway to prepare flexible and binder-free MXene@carbon dots (MXene@CDs) electrodes materials for CDI is presented, which was based on the electrostatic self-assembly between negatively charged Ti3C2Tx MXene and positively charged CDs. The 2D MXene nanosheets are bent with CDs and formed hybrid microflowers. This unique structure can reduce the self-stacking of MXene during film formation, accelerate ion transport and increase ion storage. The binder-free electrode MXene@CDs-2 shows an excellent deionization capacity of 86.4 mg/g in 10 mM NaCl solution. Moreover, the relativity low energy consumption (0.13 kWh/kg-NaCl) and high energy recovery (12.61%) are achieved. This strategy of combining zero-dimensional and two-dimensional nanomaterials to form microflowers can promote the performance of MXene-based CDI.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Capacitive deionization
Ti3C2Tx MXene
Carbon dots
Desalination
Microflowers

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Preparetion of Ti3C2Tx MXene@CDs composite films

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China ( 21974101 , 22174102 ). We thank the Dr. Yucheng Liu from the Core Facility of Wuhan University for her assistance with SEM characterization and data analysis.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Illustration of preparation of the MXene@CDs composite films. (b) Digital photographs of MXene, CDs and MXene@CDs-2 suspension (after standing 10 h). (c) Zate potential curves of MXene, CDs and MXene@CDs-2 suspension. (d) Digital photographs of MXene@CDs-2 films. The diameter of the film is about 4 cm (Fig.1d left), and the film has good flexibility (Fig. 1d right).

The SEM images of (a) MXene and (b, c) MXene@CDs-2 hybrid. The TEM images of (d) MXene and (e, f) MXene@CDs-2 hybrid. The surface (g) and cross-sectional (h, i) SEM images of MXene@CDs-2 film. (c and f are the higher magnification of the single microflower. The arrows in the Fig. 2i point to the granular microflowers.)

(a) XRD patterns of the Ti3AlC2 MAX, MXene and MXene@CDs composite films. (b) N2 adsorption/desorption isotherms (pore size distribution insert) of MXene and MXene@CDs-2. (c) XPS spectra of CDs, MXene and MXene composite films. (d) C 1s, (e) N 1s, (f) Ti 2p XPS spectra of MXene@CDs-2 film.

The results of electrochemical performance test. (a) The specific capacitance of different MXene-based films (the statistical significance analysis was processed with Ordinary one-way ANOVA-multiple comparisons on the GraphPad Prism 9, the result of * represents P ˂ 0.05, n.s. represents not significant). (b) the specific capacitance of MXene and MXene@CDs-2 at various current densities. (c) GCD and (d) CV curves of MXene@CDs-2, (e) the CV curves of MXene and MXene@CDs-2 at the scan rate of 1 mV/s. (f) Cyclic stability of the MXene@CDs-2 electrode at a scan rate of 10 mV/s for 1000 cycles. (g) The relationship of specific capacitance vs. v-1/2 and (h) the relationship of the inverse specific capacity vs. v1/2 for MXene and MXene@CDs-2 electrodes. (i) the surface-controlled and the diffusion-controlled contributions to the total capacitance of the MXene and MXene@CDs-2 electrodes.

The CDI performance test results. (a) The plots of conductivity and potential during the charging/discharging process, (b) plot of potential change versus time during desalination/regeneration. (c) Desalination capacity, (d) energy consumption and energy recovery rate under the current densities of 20, 30, 50 mA/g. (e) Desalination capacity, (f) energy consumption and energy recovery rate under voltage windows of 1.2, 1.4, 1.6 V. (g) Desalination capacity and desalination rate of MXene@CDs-2 and MXene under the initial feed NaCl concentration of 10, 30 and 40 mM. (h) Recycling performance of MXene@CDs-2 electrode at 50 mA/g.
The comparison of desalination capacity of MXene-based electrodes for CDI.

row.entry: Constant current of 20 mA/g (1.2 V voltage window)
Constant current of 50 mA/g (1.2 V voltage window)
Constant current of 20 mA/g (1.2 V voltage window)

head.ce:title: Ti3C2Tx MXene@carbon dots hybrid microflowers as a binder-free electrode material toward high capacity capacitive deionization

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Molecular Sciences, Wuhan University, Wuhan 430072, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Molecular Sciences

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry and Molecular Sciences, Wuhan University, Wuhan 430072, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Core Facility of Wuhan University, Wuhan University, Wuhan 430072, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Core Facility of Wuhan University, Wuhan University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Core Facility of Wuhan University, Wuhan University, Wuhan 430072, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Owing to the exceptional electrical conductivity and high hydrophilic surface area, MXene is regarded as one of the most glaring capacitive deionization (CDI) electrode materials. However, the self-stacking of pure MXene nanosheets though van der Waals forces seriously hinders its electrochemical performance. Therefore, a novel pathway to prepare flexible and binder-free MXene@carbon dots (MXene@CDs) electrodes materials for CDI is presented, which was based on the electrostatic self-assembly between negatively charged Ti3C2Tx MXene and positively charged CDs. The 2D MXene nanosheets are bent with CDs and formed hybrid microflowers. This unique structure can reduce the self-stacking of MXene during film formation, accelerate ion transport and increase ion storage. The binder-free electrode MXene@CDs-2 shows an excellent deionization capacity of 86.4 mg/g in 10 mM NaCl solution. Moreover, the relativity low energy consumption (0.13 kWh/kg-NaCl) and high energy recovery (12.61%) are achieved. This strategy of combining zero-dimensional and two-dimensional nanomaterials to form microflowers can promote the performance of MXene-based CDI.

ce:section.ce:para: With the deteriorating global crisis of freshwater, the research of novel methods for purification of seawater or other low salinity water has emerged as an urgent need for human survival and development[1–3]. At present, the main desalination techniques include the reverse osmosis (RO)[4], electrodialysis (ED)[5], and thermal evaporation[6]. However, these techniques present some drawbacks such as the production of secondary waste and high energy consumption[7]. Alternatively, the capacitive deionization (CDI) has aroused widespread research interest as a promising desalination method because of its environmentally friendly process, no secondary waste production and low energy consumption, which is based on the electric double layers (EDLs) and faradic reactions adsorption mechanisms[8–11]. Briefly, when a working voltage is used, the ions with opposite charges are migrated to the electrodes, and when a reverse potential is used the ions that absorbed in the electrodes are released[12]. Electrode material plays an important role in CDI. It is of great significance to design and prepare electrode materials with high capacity and outstanding electrochemical stability. Zhang et al.[13] developed MOF-on-MOF nanoarchitectures. Liu et al.[14] reported the core−shell MOF@COF motif hybridization. Wang et al.[15] fabricated the chloride pre-intercalated CoFe-layered double hydroxides. Xu et al.[16] designed the zero-dimensional carbon sphere and two-dimensional (2D) graphene forming three-dimensional (3D) frameworks and so on. Yet it is worth noting that additives are required in the process of preparing electrodes, which could affect the electrochemical performance. In order to easily prepare electrode materials and fully utilize their ion storage capacity of electrode materials, the research with binder-free and high adsorption capacity is of great significance[17–19].

The 2D MXene-based nanomaterials which possess a fascinating pseudocapacitance have aroused much attention in active electrode materials of CDI[20]. MXenes, formulated as Mn+1XnTx (M usually represents titanium or vanadium, X represents carbon or nitrogen, and T is termination group -OH, -F or -O), are a class of transition metal carbides or nitrides[21–25]. Owing to its high surface area, excellent conductivity, and reversible intercalation/de-intercalation of ions in solution, MXene is becoming a new active electrode material for the application of CDI[26–31]. Srimuk et al.[32] firstly applied pure MXene for CDI, and proved that MXene is a promising pseudocapacitive electrode material which stores charge through ion intercalation reaction, obtaining stable ion adsorption performance of 13 ± 2 mg/g. However, there are still many problems need to be solved for MXene being applied as CDI electrode material. In particular, like the other 2D nanomaterials such as graphene, MXene nanosheets emerge restacking during the electrode forming process because of van der Waals forces, which adversely affects its electrochemical performance[10,33]. Recently, there have been many reports on preventing the restacking of MXene and improving its desalination capacity and stability as CDI electrode. Bao et al.[34] introduced a porous MXene. Amiri[35] prepared nitrogen-doped Ti3C2Tx MXene. Anwer et al.[36] coated the 2D Ti3C2Tx MXene nanosheets on porous facial tissue. Ding et al.[10] synthesized MXenes-derived nitrogen-doped 3D nanofibrous architecture. And Xu et al. proposed a concept for preparing MXene-organic heterostructure to prevent restacking and oxidization of MXene such as the fabrication of 2D Ti3C2Tx MXene-polydopamine heterostructure and covalent organic framework (COF)-on-MXene heterostructure[37,38]. However, these methods limit the flexibility of MXene nanosheets to form free-standing electrodes. The binder and additives were required for the formation of CDI electrodes, which could inevitably reduce the ion adsorption capacity of electrodes and increase the cost. Ma et al.[7] applied the lithium fluoride/hydrochloric acid to etch Ti3AlC2 MAX precursor, increasing the interlayer spacing of Ti3C2Tx MXene. The free-standing film was obtained by vacuum filtration, obviating the usage of binders and additives. This binder-free electrode had a marvelous electro-sorption capacity of 67.7 mg/g. However, the pure MXene film inevitably exist restacking during the preparation of the free-standing MXene film. Thus we hope to synthesize a small-sized zero-dimensional nanomaterial as a spacer to limit the restacking of MXene nanosheets which does not affect the formation of independent MXene film. Using zero-dimensional nanomaterials as spacers for two-dimensional nanosheets has been studied in previous works about carbon spheres/graphene architecture[16]. Nevertheless, the force between the composite materials prepared by simple freeze-drying method is weak, and the polymer still needs to be added to prepare the electrode. The electrostatic self-assembly is an important method to hybridize with MXenes. For example, Yury Gogotsi`s group prepared free-standing films by electrostatic self-assembly between positively charged rGO and negatively charged MXene nanosheets as supercapacitor electrodes[33]. However, there are few reports on zero-dimensional nanomaterials hybridized with MXene nanosheets. Designing a small-scale zero-dimensional nanomaterial to combine with MXene nanosheets by electrostatic self-assembly and forming a free-standing film may be an effective means to improve the ion storage capacity of MXene in CDI.

The CDs have absorbed great attention due to their excellent properties such as outstanding conductivity and high specific surface area[39]. Additionally, doping of heteroatoms (e.g. nitrogen, phosphorus and sulfur elements) can efficiently promote the electrochemical performance of CDs by improving the electronic structures[40–43]. In this work, the positively charge N, P-doped CDs with excellent hydrophilicity were obtained through a simple one-step method in the hydrothermal reactor. Combined with MXene, the small-sized CDs may inhibit restacking of MXene, exposing more active sites. Moreover, the combination strategy may not affect MXene to form free-standing films, which can be directly used as electrodes.

Here, the zero-dimensional CDs and 2D MXene nanosheets were combined to produce the MXene@CDs hybrid by electrostatic self-assembly. In particular, the 2D MXene nanosheets and zero-dimensional CDs formed hybrid microflowers. Through vacuum filtration process, a free-standing composite MXene@CDs-2 film with good flexibility and mechanical strength was obtained. The electrodes exhibited high capacitance pseudocapacitive via the electrochemical performance test. Moreover, the electrode obtained a high desalination capacity of 86.4 mg NaCl/g MXene@CDs-2 in 10 mM NaCl solution via the performance tests of symmetric electrodes CDI cell.

The CDs were prepared according to previous reports[44,45]. 0.5 g m-phenylenediamine (aqueous 99 wt%) was added into 30 mL deionized water, followed by adding diethylenetriaminepenta (methylenephosphonicacid) (DAP, aqueous 50 wt%, 2.84 g). After that, the above mixed solution was heated at 200 °C for 300 min in 50 ml teflon autoclave. Finally, the obtained deep-brown solution was centrifuged at the rate of 10000 rpm and the supernatant was dialyzed by a 1000 MWCO of dialysis membrane. And the CDs colloid solution was obtained (1.6 mg/mL).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparetion of Ti3C2Tx MXene@CDs composite films
ce:section.ce:para: The synthesis of MXene nanosheets and chemicals were present in Text S1. 13.5 mL MXene suspension was diluted to 50 mL with deionized water, and 0.2 mL CDs suspension was added into the MXene suspension drop by drop. After being continuously magnetic stirred for 10 min, the MXene nanosheets and CDs mixing suspension was ultrasonic treatment for 10 min. The obtained composite suspension was directly filtered by vacuum filtration, and then dried in air for 24 h. Finally, the composite films were annealing treatment at 200 °C under N2 protection for 2 h, which were labeled MXene@CDs-1. The MXene@CDs-2 and MXene@CDs-3 were also prepared by the same method, the amount of carbon dots were 0.6 mL and 1.0 mL, respectively. The pure MXene films were obtained by the same way. The composite MXene films were directly used as electrodes for CDI.

The morphologies were measured by a Zeiss scanning electron microscope (SEM, Zeiss Merlin Compact, Britain) and a JEM-2100 Plus transmission electron microscope (TEM, Japan). XPert Pro X-ray diffractometer (Netherlands) was used to test the XRD patterns. The zeta potentials were characterized by the Zetasizer Nano ZSP (Britain). A specific area analyzer (Quadrasorb evoTM, USA) was applied to characterize the specific surface area. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was characterized on ESCALAB250Xi (Thermo Fisher Scientific, UK).

A CHI760E workstation was used to detect the cyclic voltammetry (CV), galvanostatic charge/discharge measurements (GCD) and Electrochemical impedance spectroscopy (EIS, an amplitude of 5 mV, frequency range from 105 Hz to 0.1 Hz) by applying a three-electrode system. The MXene-based films (1 mg, 0.5 cm2), Pt and Ag/AgCl were used as the working electrode, counter electrode and reference electrode in 1 M NaCl aqueous electrolyte, respectively. Both GCD and CV measurements can be used to detect the specific capacitance. The specific capacitance (C
s , F/g) for GCD measurement was calculated through the following equation:
(1)
C s
=
Ixt
m △ V

for CV measurement was calculated by following equation:
(2)
C s
=
∫ IdV
2 mv ∆ V
Where I (A) is the current, m (g) is the mass of electrode, t (s) is the charging time, respectively. △V (V) and v (mV/s) represent the potential window and scan rate.

The desalination performance of MXene@CDs-2 electrode was detected by a batch-mode CDI cell (Fig.S1). This device was consisted of acrylic plates, rubber gaskets, two MXene@CDs-2 film electrode (total mass of electrodes was about 17.5 mg, thickness of 13 μm), and a chamber with the size of 2 × 2 × 0.5 cm3. The feed NaCl solution (25 mL) was pumped into the CDI unit and back into the tank through a peristaltic pump. The device was cycled for 12 h before starting test, after which the power supply began to apply constant current to the CDI cell, and the conductivity change of NaCl solution was recorded in the conductivity meter (DDSJ- 308F). The flow rate was 7 mL/min. The effect of different current densities and potential window on CDI were tested (10 mM NaCl solution). And the influence of different concentrations of NaCl solution (10, 30 and 40 mM) was performed. The desalination capacity (Г) and energy consumption (W) were calculated by following equations:
(3)
Desalination capacity
Г
=
C 0
− C
x
V 0
M
(4)
Energy consumption
W
=
Ix ∫ V
dt
3.6 x
C 0
− C
x
V 0

Where C
0 and C (mg/L) represent the initial and final concentrations of NaCl, respectively. V
0 (L) and M (g) are the volume of solution and the total mass of electrodes. I (A) and V (V) represent the current and voltage, respectively.

Fig. 1a illustrates the formation of the flexible composite MXene@CDs films. The CDs were synthesized by hydrothermal method. The deep brown in Fig. 1b (left vial) is the CDs suspension, and Fig. S2 shows that the particles size of CDs is about 2.4 nm (statistics by image J software). As demonstrated in Fig.1c, the CDs possess a positive zeta potential of 36.8 mV, indicating that the positive charged function groups are carried on the CDs surface[44]. The MXene was prepared via etching Ti3AlC2 MAX powder in LiF/HCl composite solution, the resulting suspension was ultrasonicated and centrifuged to obtain MXene nanosheets suspension, which possesses a negatively zeta potential of -29.3 mV (Fig. 1c). The TEM (Fig. S3) shows that the resulting MXene is few-layered or single-layered. This indicates that MXene nanosheets have good dispersibility and hydrophilicity (Fig. 1b). When the CDs suspension was dropped into the MXene suspension, electrostatic self-assembly occurred due to the mutual neutralization of charges, CDs were attracted to the MXene nanosheets, the zeta potential of MXene suspension changed from -29.3 to -23.9 mV. After standing for ten hours, the composite suspension was separated, leaving a clear supernatant and bottom agglomerates (Fig. 1b, right vial). The MXene@CDs-2 film was obtained by vacuum filtration. For the purpose of enhancing the rates of Ti atoms by removing the most oxygen-containing functional groups to get more pseudocapacitive reaction sites, the MXene@CDs-2 film were annealed at 200 °C[26,46]. Finally, the flexible and free-standing MXene@CDs-2 film was obtained (Fig. 1d and Fig. S4).

As can be seen from the morphological characterizations observed by SEM and TEM (Fig. 2 ), the pure MXene (Fig. 2a, d) consists of clean and flexible spread nanosheets, while MXene@CDs-2 hybrid (Fig. 2b, e, c and f) is a cluster with obvious folds and microflowers structure. There are also sheet-like MXene nanosheets loaded with CDs (Fig. 2e). The microflowers structure of MXene@CDs-2 hybrid could be resulted from the electrostatic force generated by positively charged CDs to pull and deform the negatively charged MXene nanosheets. As revealed in Fig. 2f, the dark clusters may be CDs colloid bundles, and the light-colored folds are MXene nanosheets. The sheet-like and microflower-like hybrid self-assembled to form free-standing films during the filtration. The surface of the MXene-CDs-2 film shows abundant wrinkles (Fig. 2g and Fig. S5), which is good for the infiltration of water. The cross-sectional SEM was used to further characterize the morphology and structure of MXene-based films after filtration. As shown in Fig. 2h (Fig. 2i is the magnification view of Fig. 2h), the pure MXene nanosheets stacked into large well-aligned and sheet-like structures (Fig. S6). In this case, the absorbed ions need a long path to reach the active sites inside of MXene, and even some nanosheets do not exert capacitive performance. The microflowers would be formed as a result of the introduction of CDs, and then the nanosheets and flower-like hybrid self-assembled into free-standing films after vacuum filtration. It can be seen that the cross-section morphology has changed significantly. Instead, the sheets are interspersed with granular microflowers (the arrows in the Fig. 2i point to the granules) which act as spacers and formed considerable wrinkles, limiting the restacking of MXene nanosheets.

The changes of crystalline structure and composition of MXene-based films were recorded by XRD patterns. As shown in Fig.3a, when the aluminum atoms in Ti3AlC2 MAX are etched, not only most diffraction peaks of the Ti3AlC2 MAX disappear, but also the (002)-peak obviously remove to a low angle, which can also prove that the aluminum atoms are almost completely removed. The (002) diffraction peak shifts from 2θ = 6.45° for pure MXene to 6.32° for MXene@CDs-2, and the corresponding interlayer spacings are approximately 1.37 nm and 1.40 nm. The strength of the (002) peaks are also weakened due to the addition of CDs, preventing the restacking of MXene nanosheets. As shown in Fig. 3b, the N2 adsorption/desorption isotherms, the presented hysteresis loops indicate the existence of mesopores. The pore size distribution Fig. 3b insert also shows the increasing mesopores. The specific surface area, which is determined by the Brunauer-Emmett-Teller method, increased from 8.9 m2/g of MXene to 24.8 m2/g of MXene@CDs-2 hybrid. The increase of specific surface area and the mesoporous structure are beneficial to the diffusion and adsorption of ions[47].

The elemental states and composition of samples were detected by XPS. Peaks at 284, 400, 531 and 133 eV are the C, N, O and P elements, respectively. Fig. 3c shows that the CDs are basically composed of C, O, N and P elements, indicating the synthesis of N, P-doped positively charged carbon dots. With the addition of CDs, the N element appeared in the XPS spectrum of MXene composite films. The high resolution XPS spectrums of C 1s, N 1s and Ti 2p of MXene@CDs-2 were presented in Fig. 3d-f. The C 1s spectrum (Fig. 3d) shows six peaks. Peaks at 282.1eV is C-Ti, 284.2 eV is C=C, 284.7 eV is C-C, 285.3 eV is C-N/C-P, 286.2 eV is C-O, 288.2 eV is O-C=O[34,44]. The N 1s spectrum (Fig. 3e) shows three peaks. Peaks at 398.4 eV is N-H, 400.0 eV is C-N=C, 401.1 eV is N-C/N-N/N-P[44,45]. The Ti 2p spectrum (Fig. 3f) shows four pairs of peaks. Peaks at 455.1 and 461.1 eV associated to Ti-C (Ti 2p3/2 and Ti-2p1/2), 455.8 and 462.0 eV associated to Ti2+ (Ti 2p3/2 and Ti-2p1/2), 456.8 and 463.1 eV associated to Ti3+ (Ti 2p3/2 and Ti-2p1/2), 459.0 and 464.1 eV associated to Ti-O (Ti 2p3/2 and Ti-2p1/2)[7,48]. The atomic rate of N element is 1.71%, and the introduction of N atoms is beneficial to the electrochemical performance of MXene@CDs-2[35,39,49,50].

As mentioned above, with the addition of CDs, the formed hybrid microflowers and nanosheets self-assembled to form a 3D structure flexible film, which prevents the restacking of MXene and provides more diffusion paths for ions. Therefore, the MXene@CDs-2 is expected to exhibit an improved ion storage ability. Fig. 4 showed the electrochemical performance of the MXene and MXene@CDs composite films electrode. As depicted in Fig. 4a, the MXene@CDs-2 possesses the specific capacitance of 144.4 F/g, which is significantly improved comparing with the specific capacitance of pure MXene (115.6 F/g). And the statistical significance analysis also presented the significant difference between pure MXene and MXene@CDs-2, this result shows the addition of CDs in the MXene@CDs-2 well improved the specific capacitance of MXene, which benefits from the formed hybrid microflowers. The unique structure of MXene@CDs-2 can facilitate ion transportation and expose more utilizable surface area for adsorbed ions. Fig. 4b shows the specific capacitance of MXene and MXene@CDs-2 under different current densities, the specific capacitance reduces with increasing current density. However, the MXene@CDs-2 always has the highest capacitance no matter what the current density is. There is no platform in the GCD plots (Fig. 4c), indicating that the redox reaction did not occur, as well as the CV curves shown (Fig. 4d). And the good symmetry of GCD plots means good coulombic efficiency. Furthermore, the CV curve render as an irregular rectangle, which indicates the existence of pseudocapacitance except for the EDLs capacitance. The distortion degree of CV curve increases as the scan rate increases, because high-rate scan poses large polarization for the electrode material[7]. As the CV curves in Fig. 4e shown, that the current of MXene@CDs-2 is higher than that of MXene, which means that MXene@CDs-2 has significantly higher specific capacitance. The Nyquist plots and equivalent circuit were presented in Fig. S7, MXene@CDs-2 has lower charge transfer resistance (Rct) than MXene, which is more favorable for ion diffusion and electron transfer. The cycle stability of MXene@CDs-2 was performed by the CV measurement (Fig. 4f), the result shows that after 1000 cycles the MXene@CDs-2 keeps 101.1% compared with the initial specific capacitance, which shows the excellent cycle stability.

The pre-established method by Trasatti. was used to discuss the reason of the increase in the specific capacitance of MXene@CDs-2. According to this method, the current response under the certain potential can be divided into surface control (outer charge) and diffusion control[51]:
(5)
i
V
=
k 1
v +
k 2
v
1 / 2
(6)
i
V
v
1 / 2
=
k 1
v
1 / 2
+
k 2

Where the k
1
v and k
2
v 1/2 represent the contributions of surface control and diffusion control, respectively. The slope and intercept of the linear fitting result in Eq.6 represent k
1 and k
2 , respectively. Fig. 4g and 4h show the relationship of specific capacitance vs. v-1/2 and the relationship of the inverse specific capacity vs. v1/2 for electrodes. The surface control contribution and total charge were estimated from the extrapolation of v = ∞ from the plots of specific capacitance vs. v-1/2 and v = 0 from the plots of specific capacitance vs. v1/2, respectively. The results show that the contribution of surface control / diffusion control in MXene electrode was 40%/60%, and that in MXene@CDs-2 electrodes were 49%/51% (Fig. 4i), respectively, proving the dual-mode ion storage behavior of MXene@CDs-2 electrode. The increase in specific capacitance of the MXene@CDs-2 is mainly attributed to the outer charge contribution.

All the above characterizations and tests show that MXene@CDs-2 will be a good CDI electrode material for electrochemical ion storage. Therefore, the CDI performance was tested under the condition of constant current and the results are revealed in Fig. 5 . Fig. 5a shows the curves of conductivity of feed water and the potential versus time during the charge/discharge process of CDI. When a steady current is applied, the potential of the CDI unit is continuously changed, as well as the conductivity of the salt solution. When a positive current is applied, the potential of the CDI unit increases, sodium ions and chloride ions of the feed NaCl solution are captured by the cathode and anode, respectively, the conductivity of solution decreases. When a negative current is used, the sodium and chloride ions absorbed by the electrodes are released to achieve electrodes regeneration, and thus the conductivity of NaCl solution increases. This process is divided into four stages (Fig. 5b), ions are adsorbed in stage 1and 2, while ions are released in stage 3 and 4. Stage 1 and 3 are spontaneous processes due to energy recovery[52]. According to the change of conductivity obtained by the test, the average desalination capacity calculated by equation 3 are 86.4, 51.0, and 41.6 mg/g at the current densities of 20, 30, and 50 mA/g (1.2 V potential window) (Fig. 5c), respectively. The corresponding desalination rates are 0.65, 0.63 and 1.09 mg/g/min (Fig. S8), respectively. These results show that high current density obtains relatively low desalination capacity. It may contribute to the large amounts of ions adsorbed around the electrode at high current density, which increase the concentration polarization on the electrode-electrolyte interface. There was no significantly change in desalination rates with the current density increased from 20 to 30 mA/g, which shows that under low current densities, the current has little effect on the desalination rate in this CDI system. Table 1 lists the desalination capacity of MXene-based electrodes materials for CDI. The high desalination capacity of MXene@CDs-2 may be contributed to the formation of hybrid microflowers and the unique structure of composite film, so that more active sites are exposed, and more ion channels are formed. When a steady current is applied, the ions in solution can be rapidly and abundantly adsorbed to the electrodes. The energy consumption under different test conditions can be calculated by equation 4, and the energy recovery rate (%) is defined by the ratio of energy consumption in stage 1 and 3 to the one in stage 2 and 4. The average energy consumption of MXene@CDs-2 are 0.127, 0.182, 0.180 kWh/kg-NaCl, and the energy recovery rates are 12.61%, 22.82%, 22.98% (Fig. 5d) at the current densities of 20, 30, 50 mA/g, respectively. When the current density increases from 20 mA/g to 30 mA/g, the energy consumption also increases. This is because as the current density increases, the polarization of the electrodes becomes more pronounced and side reactions are more likely to occur[7]. However, When the current density increases from 30 mA/g to 50 mA/g, the energy consumption has little change, this result shows that the current density has little effect on energy consumption when the current density is higher than 30 mA/g in this CDI system, similar to CoFe-LDH/MXene CDI system[56]. Besides, the voltage window is an important factor in affecting the desalination performance. Herein, the desalination performance of the MXene@CDs-2 under the voltage windows of 1.2, 1.4, 1.6 V is tested (50 mA/g). The desalination capacity increases from 42.3 to 94.8 mg/g with the range of the voltage window increases from1.2 to 1.6 V (Fig. 5e). Fig.6f reveals the energy consumption and the energy recovery of MXene@CDs-2 at different voltage windows. The energy consumptions are 0.180, 0.187, 0.207 kWh/kg-NaCl, and the energy recovery rates are 22.99%, 5.02%, 7.45%, at the voltage windows of 1.2, 1.4 and 1.6V, respectively (the desalination rates are 1.09, 1.42 and 1.49 mg/g/min, Fig. S9). However, the desalination capacity increases accompanied by an increase of voltage window under the same current densities, the primary reason is that the higher voltage corresponded the stronger driving force, which can store more ions[57]. As can be seen in Fig. 5f, the energy consumption increases and the energy recovery rate is significantly reduced as the voltage window increases from 1.2 V to 1.4 or 1.6 V. The main reason for this is that the increase of voltage window lead to the occurrence of side reactions, such as reduction of dissolved oxygen and oxidation of Cl−[7,58,59], etc. And this part of the energy can be not recovered. However, when the voltage increase from 1.4 V to 1.6 V, the energy recovery rate increases from 5% to 7.5%, the reason for this phenomenon is that with large voltage window high desalination efficiency could be obtained (more ions could be stored), and more energy could be recycled[60,61]. Fig. 5g presents the desalination capacity and desalination rates of the MXene@CDs-2 and MXene electrodes at the feed NaCl concentrations of 10 mM to 40 mM at 50 mA/g current density 1.2 V voltage window. When the initial salt concentration was increased, the desalination rates and capacity were increased. The highest desalination capacity of 64.8 mg/g and fastest desalination rate of 2.61 mg/g/min were obtained for MXene@CDs-2 at the NaCl concentration of 40 mM. This desalination performance of MXene@CDs-2 electrode is excellent than the pure MXene and many of reported electrode materials (Table 1). Fig. 5h shows the desalination capacity test result of 35 cycles, the MXene@CDs-2 still maintains 86.6% of the initial capacity, which proved its cycle stability.

In summary, we have obtained flexible and binder-free MXene@CDs electrode materials by hybridizing electrostatic self-assembly of Ti3C2Tx MXene nanosheets and CDs. The interaction of CDs and MXene facilitated the formation of hybrid microflowers in MXene@CDs-2, which weakened the restacking of MXene nanosheets during the preparation of film. When being employed as the electrodes of CDI device, the MXene@CDs-2 electrode exhibited significantly higher desalination capacity (86.4 mg/g at 20 mA/g), and low energy consumption (0.13 kWh/kg-NaCl) with an energy recovery rate of 12.61%. This strategy sets a good example for reducing the self-stacking of 2D materials and preparing free-binder electrode films to achieve high desalination capacity for CDI applications. Our work may advance the application of MXene composite electrodes materials for CDI.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (21974101, 22174102). We thank the Dr. Yucheng Liu from the Core Facility of Wuhan University for her assistance with SEM characterization and data analysis.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.desal.2022.116267.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Two-Dimensional molybdenum carbide (MXene) with divacancy ordering for brackish and seawater desalination via cation and anion intercalation

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: P. Srimuk, J. Halim, J. Lee, Q. Tao, J. Rosen, V. Presser, Two-Dimensional molybdenum carbide (MXene) with divacancy ordering for brackish and seawater desalination via cation and anion intercalation, ACS Sustain. Chem. Eng. 6 (2018) 3739-3747. 10.1021/acssuschemeng.7b04095.
